text: The surgeon introduces a V-care manipulator to mobilize the uterus. The round ligament is sectioned at an intermediate point between the lateral pelvic wall and the uterus. The ovarian vessels are then sectioned, with the ovary pulled upward and lateralized to identify the ureter two centimeters below, allowing safe coagulation of the ovarian vessels near the ovary.
The surgeon dissects the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, separating both leaves to identify the uterine artery. This procedure is then repeated on the opposite side of the pelvis.

The surgeon sections the basic uterine peritoneum by pushing the uterus inward and pulling the anterior peritoneum upward, making a transverse incision one centimeter below the uterine flexor. Blunt dissection is used to open the vesicouterine space, with careful attention due to potential adhesions from previous cesarean sections.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine arteries at the lateralmost point of the uterus, coinciding with the cap of the uterine manipulator. The lumen of the uterine artery is visible during this process. The contralateral uterine artery is similarly coagulated and sectioned.

The surgeon performs a circumferential colporomy, starting with the anterior colporomy one centimeter from the bladder to prepare the anterior vaginal margin. During the lateral colporomy, bleeding is observed. The uterosacral ligament is coagulated and sectioned.
The surgeon removes the surgical specimen through the vagina, followed by the closure of the vaginal cuff using two X-points sutures in each vaginal angle with Vicryl-II-O.
The surgeon completes the suture of the vaginal cuff by bringing the X points together at the midline, providing a final view of the pelvis at the end of the hysterectomy procedure.
